category_title: At-risk middle school students in STEM service-learning projects
minimal_context: The student is in science class planning a project to test local water sources and create information sheets.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Today in science class, we need to go over the steps for testing wells used for drinking water, as well as all city systems, creeks, rivers, and ponds.

STUDENT: (Shows a lack of time on task, staring away from the desk) I'm not doing this science stuff.

TUTOR: We need to focus so we can create the information sheets reporting to the individuals whose water will be tested.

STUDENT: (Continues to stay off task and talks loudly to another student) Who cares about the city systems and ponds?

TUTOR: Please look at the assignment and start working.

STUDENT: (Pushes the papers off the desk and stands up) I said I'm not doing it! Just give me a referral to the office for discipline, I don't care!

options.A: Explicitly incorporate and promote self-compassion within the content of the science project and the resulting information sheets.
options.B: Facilitate project-based learning experiences dealing with real-life applications of the principles and experiments the student encounters in the science project.
options.C: Confront the student's misbehavior and indifference toward the science project and the water testing assignment with stern lectures.
options.D: Combine photo elicitation with life-story splicing, having the student share photographs and stories of their family during the science project.
